chunk_summary: Guidelines for macronutrient decisions in toddlers aged 1–3 years emphasize energy-dense meals, adequate fat, high-quality protein, and avoiding excessive milk or snacks. Practical feeding strategies and escalation criteria for poor growth or feeding issues are outlined.
learning_objectives: Understand the macronutrient needs and feeding strategies for toddlers aged 1–3 years.
Identify pitfalls in toddler nutrition, such as excessive milk or grazing on snacks.
Learn practical interventions for poor appetite and feeding disorders in toddlers.
Recognize when to escalate care based on growth or feeding concerns.
key_facts: Toddlers require 3 meals and 2 planned snacks daily, with water offered between meals.
Avoid low-fat diets in toddlers unless medically indicated; add energy with oil, ghee, or peanut paste.
Include at least one high-quality protein source daily, such as milk, egg, fish, or cereal–pulse combinations.
Excessive milk intake can displace solids; limit milk to avoid it becoming the main calorie source.
Escalate care if weight-for-height is < -2 SD or > +2 SD, or if feeding disorders are suspected.
reference_quote: ‘Milk is nutritious, but too much makes the stomach full and reduces meals. We will shift milk to fixed times, stop bottle feeding, and offer family foods first; weight gain usually improves in 2–4 weeks.’
document_key_topics: Macronutrient needs in toddlers aged 1–3 years
Escalation criteria for growth or feeding concerns
